texts.full_text: Div.className = 'myText'; textDiv.style = Object.entries(css).map(([k, v]) => `${k}:${v}`).join(';'); document.body.appendChild(textDiv); } ``` This function sets the text's color and applies the styling to the chart. The chart is dynamically refreshing values going from JSON and series is updated by command: chart.series[0].setData(incoming_value); After this line, when I'm calling the function: setText('myText', 0.3, colorParameter); Each refresh generate text added on top of text so it's generate new lines

texts.full_text: other countries with supreme courts found a way to reduce this polarization within the court? Note: the two examples are about Republicans just because that’s what I could think of, not because I think Democrats couldn’t have done something similar. A: In the United Kingdom, the process for nominating a judge to the Supreme Court starts with the Court itself forming a commission, almost entirely made up of legal professionals (judges, etc.) Once the Commission has consulted those it is required to consult, it will make a recommendation to the Lord Chancellor (who is usually also the Secretary of State for Justice). This process was designed in part as an attempt at insulating judges from direct political influence. The current system requires that nominees be "independent and objective" while taking into account their professional qualities, expertise related specifically with justice work or judicial office (such qualifications are not typically considered politically biased), the need for diversity within appointments to maintain public confidence on matters of fairness around who sits in judgment over us all; it's about choosing individuals based upon how good they would be at doing a job rather than aligning themselves closely towards any particular side’re interested parties migh
